data.title: Bondi's favorability rating hit new lows before her ouster
data.description:
Former Attorney General Pam Bondi was losing favor among Americans across the political spectrum in the months before President Trump fired her on Thursday. 

Bondi’s net favorability sank to -26 percentage points in mid-February — with 23 percent of the country viewing her favorably and 49 percent viewing her unfavorably — and remained at -25 percentage points in early March, according to YouGov and The Economist surveys.

That new baseline marked a stark departure from polling throughout 2025. The last time YouGov and The Economist polled Bondi’s favorability was in mid-October, when her net favorability was -13 percentage points — with 26 percent viewing her favorably and 39 percent unfavorably.

The former attorney general began Trump’s second term with a net +2 percentage point favorability rating, but it slowly began to slide, hitting -5 percentage points in February 2025 and -12 percentage points in April 2025.

Before the latest polling from this year, Bondi’s previous low was recorded in mid-July, when she fell under sharp scrutiny for a Justice Department memo saying there was no evidence convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein kept a so-called “client list” — despite saying a few months earlier that the list was “sitting on my desk right now.” The memo also reaffirmed that Epstein died by suicide.

That month, Bondi’s net favorability slipped to its previous record of -18 percentage points, but it rebounded a couple weeks later, ticking up to -14 percentage points in late July.

Throughout Bondi’s tenure at DOJ, the share of the American public with a favorable view of her stayed relatively stable — with 26 percent viewing her favorably in January 2025 and 23 percent viewing her favorably in March 2026. 

But the share of Americans who saw her unfavorably doubled — from 24 percent when Trump took office to 48 percent last month. 

Bondi’s net favorability rating has declined across the board — with Democrats, Republicans and independents all showing steep drops in the last few months. 

Among Republicans, Bondi’s net favorability in January 2025 stood at +41 percentage points — with 48 percent seeing her favorably and 7 percent unfavorably. After a dip in July to +30 percentage points, Bondi’s net favorability among Republicans ticked back up to +38 percentage points by October 2025. 

Since then, however, Bondi’s net favorability has taken a 10-point dive among the GOP, with a new low of net +28 percentage point favorability recorded in both February and March of this year. 

Among Democrats, meanwhile, Bondi held a net -35 percentage point favorability rating at the start of the Trump administration and that declined over the year to hit -58 percentage points by October 2025. But in the few months since then, Democrats have soured even more on the former attorney general, whose net favorability sank another 17 points to net -75 percentage points in the 2026 polls.

Independents began Trump’s term somewhat split on Bondi, whose net favorability stood at +1 percentage points at the time. That has continued to slide, dropping to -20 percentage points in October 2025, before dropping another 9 points to -29 percentage points in the latest polling.

Bondi is the second high-profile public firing Trump has carried out in recent weeks. Kristi Noem was replaced as secretary of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) in early March.

Trump praised Bondi as “a Great American Patriot and a loyal friend, who faithfully served as my Attorney General over the past year,” in a post on Truth Social Thursday.

“We love Pam, and she will be transitioning to a much needed and important new job in the private sector, to be announced at a date in the near future, and our Deputy Attorney General, and a very talented and respected Legal Mind, Todd Blanche, will step in to serve as Acting Attorney General,” Trump added.

Blanche, a former federal prosecutor, worked as Trump’s personal defense attorney in cases brought by the Justice Department under former President Biden.

data.title: House Republicans seek to fund ICE before passing Senate DHS bill, complicating plan to end shutdown
data.description:
House GOP leaders have a tough task ahead in passing a two-step plan to end the record-setting Department of Homeland Security (DHS) shutdown as their members call for passage of a Republican-only bill to fund immigration enforcement before they accept a bipartisan Senate bill to fund the rest of the department.

House Republicans are feeling whiplash and on a closed conference call Thursday they fumed over pressure to pass the Senate bill. That measure, which has already passed the upper chamber via unanimous consent would fund the bulk of DHS outside of Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and Border Patrol, leaving those in a GOP-only reconciliation bill to pass by June 1. Democrats have refused to vote for ICE and border patrol funding unless they get significant reforms to immigration enforcement.

Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) last week slammed the Senate bill as a “joke,” and the House GOP responded by passing a stopgap to fund the department for eight weeks. But with the stopgap unable to overcome a Senate filibuster, Johnson joined with Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) and Trump to publicly back the two-step plan on Thursday.

Republicans going well beyond usual leadership antagonists in the House Freedom Caucus said on the Thursday conference call that they would not vote for the Senate bill to fund the bulk of DHS until Congress passes a budget reconciliation bill that includes funding for ICE and Border Patrol, sources told The Hill.

Members want to see assurances that an immigration enforcement and border security bill that can pass through the special budget reconciliation process — which bypasses the Senate’s 60-vote threshold and the need for support from Democrats — will come to fruition. 

“There’s no desire to pass the Senate open borders bill and then hope that we get a reconciliation bill that would close the border. They’ve got to come together,” Rep. Randy Fine (R-Fla.) told The Hill. “We have the Senate open borders bill. Now, they’ve got to send over the one that will keep the border closed.”

That pushback means House GOP leaders might not have the votes to pass the Senate DHS bill. Going through a normal process would require near-unanimous support from Republicans for a procedural rule vote. 

The other option, an expedited suspension of the rules process, requires support from two-thirds of the House. While Democratic leaders are supportive of the Senate bill, it is not clear enough Republicans would support it to meet that threshold. And politically, GOP leaders are wary of the “Hastert rule,” a practice that calls for not bringing up any bill that does not get support from a majority of Republicans.

Some conservatives stated that position and aired their frustrations publicly. 

“Let’s make this simple: caving to Democrats and not paying CBP and ICE is agreeing to defund Law Enforcement and leaving our borders wide open again. If that’s the vote, I’m a NO,” Rep. Scott Perry (R-Pa.), a former Freedom Caucus chair, wrote on X on Wednesday.

If frustrated Republicans block quick passage of the Senate bill or GOP leaders agree to wait for a reconciliation bill, it could be weeks or months before critical agencies in DHS are funded.

The Senate bill to fund the bulk of DHS — including the Transportation Security Administration (TSA), Coast Guard, and Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) — is back in the House’s court, having passed again by unanimous consent in the Senate early Thursday morning. The House did not attempt to pass the bill in a pro forma session shortly after that on Thursday.

Both chambers are out on recess until the week of April 13, with some members traveling internationally. GOP leaders indicated on the call that there is not a plan to bring the House back early to pass the Senate bill, a source said.

Trump relieved some of the pressure to pass immediate DHS funding with his announcement on Thursday that he would sign a measure to pay all DHS employees. He had previously signed an order to pay all TSA agents, and Border Patrol and ICE agents have also been receiving pay during the shutdown.

DHS funding won’t be the only headache for GOP leaders when they do return. Republican leaders are also trying to pass a clean extension of Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act (FISA) spy powers before the current authorization expires on April 20. House GOP leaders have already once delayed plans to pass the reauthorization due to pushback from hardliners seeking reforms to the program or unrelated voting reforms as part of the bill.

The pressure from House Republicans holding up the Senate bill could affect the scope of a second reconciliation bill to fund ICE and Border Patrol.

Johnson and Thune in a statement on Wednesday had pointed out that Sen. Lindsay Graham (R-S.C.), chair of the Senate Budget Committee, in March announced his panel would start moving on a reconciliation bill to fund ICE removal operations, enact elements of voting reform legislation and fund the war in Iran. Other Republicans, mostly in the House, have called to use a reconciliation bill to address fraud in federal programs and cut spending.

But Thune indicated on Thursday that an ICE and Border Patrol reconciliation bill might not include other priorities in order to move it more quickly.

“Everybody is, I think, singularly focused …. around the things we have to do on the border, on ICE and CBP [Customs and Border Protection],” Thune told reporters. “The other things implicate other committees and create jurisdictional challenges and germane issues on the floor. Our theory of the case behind all this was to keep that thing as narrow and focused as possible, and that maximizes, I think, the speed at which we can do it and the support for it.”

Reconciliation can be used once per fiscal year, meaning Republicans could theoretically use the process two more times this year.

Sudiksha Kochi contributed.

data.title: Hegseth ousts Army chief of staff
data.description:
Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth asked the Army’s chief of staff, Gen. Randy George, to step down from the post and retire immediately, a Pentagon official told The Hill on Thursday. 

The Hill has reached out to the Army for comment. 

George assumed the role, which is typically a four-year post, in September 2023 after being confirmed by the Senate following most recently serving as the Army’s vice chief of staff. George, a career infantry officer who graduated from West Point, was nominated by former President Biden. 

On Tuesday, Hegseth said the Army crew that flew two AH‑64 Apache helicopters near Kid Rock’s Nashville home will not face suspension and ended the investigation into the service members. Earlier that day, the crew were suspended by the Army and the service opened an investigation into the incident. 

George’s ouster was reported earlier by CBS News.

DEVELOPING

data.title: Democrats warn of dire humanitarian crisis in Cuba under Trump's blockade
data.description:
Dozens of congressional Democrats sent a letter to President Trump on Thursday raising urgent alarm over an escalating humanitarian crisis in Cuba, made worse, they said, by the administration’s fuel quarantine against the island. 

A letter, obtained exclusively by The Hill, was signed by 52 House and Senate Democrats and led by Rep. Gregory Meeks (D-N.Y.), the ranking member of the House Foreign Affairs Committee, and Sen. Tim Kaine (D-Va.), the ranking member of the Senate Foreign Relations subcommittee on the Western Hemisphere.

“Doubling down on failed strategies by restricting access to energy and health care is contrary to American values and is needlessly exacerbating a humanitarian crisis,” the lawmakers wrote. 

“By engineering an accelerated energy collapse, your administration has shifted responsibility for Cuba’s suffering away from the Cuban government and squarely onto the United States.”

It comes as Trump is signaling a softening on his effective two-month oil blockade against the island. The White House said Monday that energy shipments to the island will be evaluated on a “case-by-case” basis, after it allowed a Russian tanker to deliver fuel to Cuba. 

Still, Democrats criticize Trump’s pressure campaign against Cuba, which includes blocking the majority of fuel needed for the island, which is on top of a long-standing federal trade embargo.

The lawmakers argue these policies have only caused harm to the estimated 10 million island residents and done nothing to change the behavior of the 67-year-old communist regime. 

The Democrats said they are willing to work with Trump to modify “draconian and outdated U.S. sanctions.” 

“The only U.S. policy that can help Cuba chart a brighter future is one that empowers its people, not uses them as pawns in a strategy that has consistently fallen short,” they wrote.

“Cuba has signaled its willingness to cooperate on issues you have claimed to be priorities, namely migration and drug trafficking, and we urge you to pursue diplomacy over failed pressure tactics.”

Trump imposed a fuel quarantine against Cuba at the end of January, in the aftermath of the U.S. military operation capturing Venezuela’s president, Nicolas Maduro. The U.S. took over Venezuela’s oil industry and halted oil shipments to the island that Havana had relied on for the majority of its needs.

In announcing the operation to capture Maduro, Trump predicted Cuba’s communist regime would collapse. Addressing his pressure campaign on the island, Trump has described that he will have “the honor” of taking Cuba, adding he can do whatever he wants with it. At the beginning of his second term, Trump reimposed a designation on Cuba as a State Sponsor of Terrorism. 

Secretary of State Marco Rubio is leading talks with Cuba’s government, reportedly in touch with the grandson of former president and defense minister, 94-year-old Raul Castro, viewed as the center of power. 

The administration has not telegraphed their exact demands for Cuba’s government, but Rubio has criticized Cuban leaders as failing to make the necessary political and economic reforms to warrant an easing of the blockade. The Libertad Act of 1996, also called the Helms-Burton Act, conditions a transition to democracy, strengthening the rule of law, and guaranteeing fundamental freedoms as necessary to receive relief on economic sanctions.

Lawmakers, critical of the president’s maximum pressure campaign, are also warning the administration against launching any military operation against the island, saying such a move would be illegal and a waste of American taxpayer dollars. 

“The United States cannot bomb Cuba out of economic collapse and political repression,” they wrote. 

Decades of mismanagement, devastation wrought by Hurricane Melissa in October and increased pressure from Trump’s fuel quarantine have pushed the country to the brink. The island has suffered three island-wide blackouts over the course of March. While the government has acknowledged talks with the U.S. and announced reforms, Rubio has criticized these as not going far enough. 

Among the humanitarian toll, the United Nations said recently that 50,000 surgeries had to be postponed in February because of a lack of resources. Humanitarian aid is stalled in warehouses without fuel for trucks to deliver it, garbage is piling up on the streets and air quality is deteriorating as people burn waste and food for cooking. 

On March 24, the U.N. presented a $94 million Plan of Action to assist 4.2 million people most affected, covering health, water and sanitation, food security, education, housing and protection.

data.title: Vance to visit Hungary, meet with Viktor Orbán
data.description:
Vice President Vance will head to Hungary next week to hold bilateral meetings with Prime Minister Viktor Orbán and give remarks on the partnership between the U.S. and Hungary.

Second lady Usha Vance will be traveling with the vice president when he goes from April 7-8, according to a statement by Vance’s office.

The trip comes just days before Hungary’s election.

President Trump has already endorsed Orbán in a lengthy social media post last month, calling him “highly respected [and a] truly strong and powerful leader with a proven track record of delivering phenomenal results.”

“He fights tirelessly for, and loves, his Great Country and People, just like I do for the United States of America,” the president wrote. “Viktor works hard to Protect Hungary, Grow the Economy, Create Jobs, Promote Trade, Stop Illegal Immigration, and Ensure LAW AND ORDER!”

Trump said he and Orbán have “reached new heights of cooperation and spectacular achievement” during his second term.

Orban has been the leader of Hungary for two decades. Trump has previously endorsed him in 2020 and 2022.

The Hungarian prime minister traveled to Washington in November, and the conversation mostly surrounded Orbán’s request to continue getting oil from Russia after the U.S. placed sanctions on the export.

But it was also something of a lovefest, with the leaders trading compliments back and forth.

“What we need is a golden age,” Orbán said at the time. “To open a golden age of the United States-Hungary relationship.”

Secretary of State Marco Rubio visited Budapest last month where he spoke about the two countries’ relations.

data.title: 'Good riddance': Lawmakers sound off on Trump's Bondi ouster
data.description:
President Trump announced that he had fired Attorney General Pam Bondi on Thursday, a move that followed months of controversy surrounding the Justice Department’s handling of files linked to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

The ouster was welcomed by many lawmakers on both sides of the aisle and also comes shortly after the firing of another Trump Cabinet member, former Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem.

“Good riddance. Pam Bondi was the wrong choice from the start,” Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) said in a post on the social platform X in response to Bondi’s firing.

“But the rot at the Department of Justice begins and ends with Donald Trump. As long as his focus is on using DOJ as a tool for revenge and not law enforcement, the cover up of the Epstein files, along with the countless other problems at DOJ, will continue,” he added.

Sen. Sheldon Whitehouse (D-R.I.) said in his own X post that “MAGA Attorney General Pam Bondi cashed in her integrity to keep her job and now leaves with neither.”

“It’s a lesson for people who fall for Trump,” he added.

While Bondi was at the helm of the Justice Department, it went through multiple notable losses as prosecutors failed to successfully mount cases against numerous Trump enemies. 

Shortly after Bondi entered office, she also garnered backlash from the Republican base by organizing a White House meeting to talk about the Epstein files, in which she mostly released information that was already public.

Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.), who has been a major advocate for the release of files linked to Epstein, also celebrated Bondi’s ouster.

“I support Trump firing Pam Bondi. Do you?” Massie said in an X post. “I hope the next AG will release all the Epstein files according to the law and follow up with investigations, prosecutions, and arrests.”

Massie also included a poll in his post, with “He was right to fire her” and “He should have kept her” as the two choices users could pick from.

Rep. Lauren Boebert (R-Colo.) appeared to play off of previous comments Bondi had made during a February House Judiciary Committee hearing about the stock market in response to her firing.

“DOW fell below 50,000?” Boebert said in an X post.

House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) also offered commentary on Bondi’s ouster in an X post, saying, “Pam Bondi has been fired. Good riddance.”

The Hill has reached out to the White House and Justice Department for comment.

data.title: Border officials violated warrantless arrests order: Judge
data.description:
A federal judge in California ruled on Wednesday that federal agents violated a previous court order restricting them from making immigration arrests without a warrant during an enforcement operation in Sacramento last summer.

Judge Jennifer Thurston found last April that U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) agents had engaged in a “pattern and practice of warrantless arrests” and barred them from conducting stops without reasonable suspicion that the person was in the country illegally or probable cause of flight risk.   

In a 63-page order released Wednesday, Thurston claimed her order was violated when Border Patrol agents performed immigration enforcement in the parking lot of a Home Depot in July 2025.

Thurston, a Biden appointee, said agents used “eleven, virtually identical” forms issued by the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) to support the detention and arrests of individuals in that operation.

“Having carefully examined the entire record, including the recorded footage of the Sacramento action, the Court finds that Defendants have again detained people without reasonable suspicion for doing so,” she wrote, adding that that agents relied on “unsupported assumptions, hunches and generalizations about the relationship between a person’s apparent status as a day laborer and their immigration status” rather than individualized assessments.  

Thurston ordered Border Patrol agents throughout the Eastern District of California to document their reasoning and circumstances surrounding future immigration stops.  

Judges in Oregon, Colorado and Washington, D.C., have issued similar orders in the past year, as the Trump administration continues to pursue its sweeping immigration crackdown.  

The tactics employed by federal law enforcement officers in immigration raids have come under intense scrutiny since President Trump returned to office, with civil rights groups accusing CBP and Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) of racial profiling.

The use of administrative warrants, rather than standard judicial warrants, to forcibly enter a home and arrest immigrants has also been a point of contention.

In February, Sen. Richard Blumenthal (D-N.Y.) argued the internal document functioned as a “permission slip” to violate someone’s Fourth Amendment protections against unreasonable searches and seizures.

DHS officials have previously defended the practice by arguing that it is only used when an immigrant without proper documentation is on a final order of removal from a judge. Newly appointed Secretary Markwayne Mullin seemed to support the idea of needing judicial warrants during his confirmation hearing, however.

data.title: Moulton: Trump 'resorting to war crimes' to get out of Iran war
data.description:
Democratic Rep. Seth Moulton (Mass.) on Thursday said President Trump is “resorting to war crimes” to end the U.S. military operations against Iran. 

The president threatened to decimate Iran’s energy infrastructure and to send them “back to the stone ages” in an address to the nation on Wednesday evening. 

“He’s kicked the hornets nest, he’s got his foot stuck in it and he doesn’t know how to get out. So what is he doing? He’s resorting to war crimes,” Moulton said in a Thursday interview on MS NOW. 

Moulton, who sits on the House Armed Services Committee, condemned reported attacks on Iranian civilian infrastructure. 

“This is the same stuff that we criticize [Russian President] Vladimir Putin for doing in Ukraine,” Moulton said. “For years, we’ve attacked Putin because he’s the one who’s taking out civilian infrastructure in Ukraine.”

Moulton accused Trump of having “no plan to end this war that he started and is now losing.”

“He is losing this war of choice that he began in Iran,” the Democrat continued. 

The senator also addressed the president’s recent announcement that Pam Bondi would be departing her role as attorney general. Trump said Bondi would be “transitioning to a much needed and important new job in the private sector” and appointed Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche as the interim leader of the Justice Department. 

Moulton slammed Bondi as “an extremist,” and he pointed to the attorney general’s upcoming scheduled testimony before the House Oversight Committee about her department’s handling of the release of files related to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. 

“She was about to be deposed in the Epstein case. That’s why Trump got rid of her, 12 days before that was supposed to happen, and he’s put in place someone who did a sweetheart deal for him with regards to Epstein, the guy who went down and interviewed Ghislaine Maxwell and got her out of a maximum security prison,” Moulton said, referring to Blanche. 

Oversight Ranking Member Robert Garcia (D-Calif.) said he still intends to press for Bondi’s testimony. 

“She will not escape accountability and remains legally obligated to appear before our Committee under oath,” Garcia wrote in a social media post. “She must answer for her mishandling of the Epstein files and the special treatment she has given Ghislaine Maxwell.”

data.title: What to know about Todd Blanche, acting AG after Bondi firing
data.description:
Todd Blanche will take over the Justice Department in an acting capacity following President Trump’s ouster of Attorney General Pam Bondi. 

It’s the highest perch yet for the former New York prosecutor and white-collar defense attorney, who came into Trump’s orbit as one of his personal lawyers until the president catapulted Blanche into the Justice Department. 

In his announcement on Thursday, Trump called Blanche a “very talented and respected Legal Mind.”

Here’s what to know about Blanche as he becomes acting attorney general. 

Blanche gave up prestigious law firm job to represent Trump 

Before joining the Justice Department, Blanche represented Trump in several of his criminal prosecutions. 

He gave up a lot professionally for it. 

Blanche had worked as a partner at Cadwalader, Wickersham & Taft. It’s now one of the largest law firms in the world after merging with Hogan Lovells. 

Blanche has long lamented how his colleagues forced him to give up his partnership to take on Trump as a client. 

Appearing on the “For The Defense with David Oscar Markus” podcast in 2024, Blanche said he was treated differently than lawyers who represented Jeffrey Epstein and Hunter Biden. 

“They can have Big Law represent them and the Big Law lawyers get awards, they get to go to galas, they get to speak, they’re posting on the LinkedIn how amazing they are,” Blanche said. 

“But that if you’re representing somebody like the former president of the United States in a white-collar case, that I have to leave my law firm,” he added. 

Trump originally hired Blanche to defend him upon being indicted on 34 criminal charges in Manhattan stemming from a 2016 hush money payment to an adult film star. 

He ultimately served as Trump’s lead attorney in that trial, which culminated in a guilty verdict. Blanche also defended Trump against the now-defunct election subversion and classified documents prosecutions brought by Special Counsel Jack Smith. 

Once a registered Democrat in New York, Blanche has long expressed agreement with Trump that the justice system was treating him unfairly. He later became a registered Republican in Florida and even attended the 2024 Republican National Convention. 

Trump brings on Blanche at DOJ 

As the president would sometimes fire other members of his team as his legal entanglements progressed, Trump stuck with Blanche.

By the time Trump won his second White House term, he had become one of Trump’s most prominent personal lawyers. 

The president then nominated Blanche to serve as his No. 2 in the Justice Department. It’s a key role for the president, who soured on many of the senior DOJ officials he appointed during his first term. 

The Senate confirmed Blanche in a 52-46 party-line vote. Democrats refused to vote for him over concerns he’d help the president “weaponize” the department. 

Since joining the administration, Blanche has been part of massive reworkings of the Justice Department, including its purges of prosecutors who worked in Smith’s special counsel office and other Jan. 6 prosecutions.

“There is not a single man or woman at the Department of Justice who had anything to do with those prosecutions,” Blanche boasted last month at the Conservative Political Action Conference.

Last year, Blanche spearheaded the Justice Department’s talks with Ghislaine Maxwell, Epstein’s longtime accomplice who remains in prison. And in recent months, Blanche has helped defend the department’s response to the new law requiring the release of the Epstein files. Blanche has said the DOJ’s review is “over.”

The Justice Department has also launched various criminal probes into Trump’s political adversaries, including New York Attorney General Letitia James (D) and former FBI Director James Comey.

Blanche personally signed the court filings advocating for the controversial dismissal of then-New York City Mayor Eric Adams’s (D) criminal corruption case. Several career prosecutors had resigned after refusing to sign the documents. 

Blanche’s roots as New York prosecutor 

It was a full circle moment for Blanche, who had spent nearly a decade as a prosecutor in that office, the U.S. attorney for the Southern District of New York. 

He joined the office in 2006 and at one point served as its co-chief of the violent crimes unit. 

Blanche left in 2014 to join the law firm WilmerHale. He moved to Cadwalader about three years later.

Blanche graduated from Brooklyn Law School and clerked for then-U.S. District Judge Denny Chin.

“Thank you to President Trump for the trust and the opportunity to serve as Acting Attorney General,” Blanche wrote on social media on Thursday. “We will continue backing the blue, enforcing the law, and doing everything in our power to keep America safe.”

data.title: Massie raises Epstein files in X poll after Bondi ousted
data.description:
Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.), one of the lead lawmakers behind the push to release the Jeffrey Epstein files, wants to know how the public feels about Pam Bondi being ousted as attorney general.  

“I support Trump firing Pam Bondi. Do you?” Massie posed to his followers on the social platform X on Thursday afternoon.

“I hope the next AG will release all the Epstein files according to the law and follow up with investigations, prosecutions, and arrests,” he added.

At the bottom of the post was a poll with two options: “He was right to fire her” or “he should have kept her.”

It racked up nearly 6,500 votes within an hour, with an overwhelming majority of respondents agreeing that President Trump was “right” to let Bondi go from the Justice Department.

Massie co-authored the Epstein Files Transparency Act alongside Rep. Ro Khanna (D-Calif.), which required the Justice Department to release unredacted files related to the investigation into Epstein and his associate Ghislaine Maxwell.  

He regularly criticized Bondi for how her department handled the disclosures, accusing her of engaging in a cover-up and failing to adequately comply with the law by flouting deadlines, withholding and over-redacting documents and exposing the identities of victims.

In one particularly tense February clash, Massie suggested during a House Judiciary Committee hearing that the DOJ only unredacted the email of someone “likely incriminated” in the documents because the department had been caught “red-handed.”

Bondi later responded by calling the Kentucky Republican a “failed politician” who has “Trump derangement syndrome,” pressing him on why lawmakers did not focus on the Epstein probe under prior administrations.

“This cover-up spans decades, and you are responsible for this portion of it,” Massie responded.

Trump announced on Thursday that Bondi would be leaving her role as attorney general for a job in the private sector, with Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche taking over in an acting capacity.

Bondi’s exit is not expected to shield her from a planned deposition before the House Oversight and Government Reform Committee, which voted to subpoena her in March over the Epstein files.

“She will not escape accountability and remains legally obligated to appear before our Committee under oath,” the top Democrat on the panel, Rep. Robert Garcia (Calif.), said in a statement, in part. “She must answer for her mishandling of the Epstein files and the special treatment she has given Ghislaine Maxwell.”
